title: monkbent.net – A personal blog by Ben Thompson
textSnippet: monkbent.net – A personal blog by Ben Thompson Skip to content monkbent.net A personal blog by Ben Thompson UHO: Audio July 4, 2020 This is part of a series about the Ultimate Home Office (UHO) . My audio setup is very important to me: on one hand, I have multiple podcasts, including Exponent , Dithering , and the Stratechery Daily Update Podcast , which means input matters, and I also listen to (non-vocal) music while writing, which means output matters. And, of course, there are calls and conferences. Recording Mic(s) My primary recording mic is the Shure BETA 87A Supercardioid Condenser Vocal Microphone . Condenser microphones generally have better sound quality but are more unforgiving of surrounding noise than dynamic microphones; this mic, though, rejects almost all sound that is not straight on, making it ideal for a home office where there may sometimes be sounds you can’t control. I also like that it has a built in shock mount; external ones look cool, but just get in the way if you want to be able to actually use your computer while recording. For travel I use the Audio-Technica ATR2100x-USB Cardioid Dynamic Microphone , which is the updated version of the ATR

Wissner-Gross is a physicist, computer scientist, entrepreneur, and investor working at the intersection of artificial intelligence and the physical world. He is Founder and Chief Scientist of Physical Superintelligence (PSI) , and has backed and advised more than 40 technology companies, both directly and through 021T and Reified . He co-hosts Moonshots , one of the most-watched technology podcasts in the world, and writes The Innermost Loop , a daily newsletter reaching more than 100,000 readers. He is the author of Solve Everything and a contributing author of the New York Times Science Bestseller This Idea Must Die and the Amazon #1 New Release What to Think About Machines That Think . His TED talks have been viewed over 2 million times in 27 languages. He has received 128 major distinctions , authored 24 publications , and been granted 26 patents . textSnippet: Who is Justin Liao? Who is Justin Liao? Home Home Who is Justin Liao? Scroll Hi, I’m Justin. I am a Miami/NYC-based Cartographer of Connection, Curiosity Architect, and Luck Engineer. My forté is generating serendipity and connecting the dots - nothing fires me up more than bringing people and ideas together to catalyze business, technology, and culture. I’m obsessed with cultivating communities and solving weird problems. Currently, I am at Praxis . Praxis is a Society-as-a-Service company, building a new city with our community. The thesis is simple; our brightest minds are working on exiting Earth to either Mars or the Metaverse. Humanity was born on this planet and we are shamefully letting it decay. We can try to escape inward, we can try to escape outward. Or, we can oblige our duty to build a beautiful future on Earth. In pursuit of our community’s new home, we are actively engaging with landowners and governments, designing economic and political systems, architecting buildings and infrastructure, and bringing together the people who will inhabit them. My life's mission is to create more spaces for curious and ambitious people to meet. Instagram X LinkedIn Newsletter Events MY STORY After living in China for 20 years, I moved to America to work for Facebook. Shortly after, I went to Google to build products, and within a year, I left to throw "tech" parties. Along the way I invested in over 20 startups. I never imagined this could be a real job. Over 50,000 CEOs, investors, and tech professionals across eight cities have attended my events, and we’ve been sponsored by institutions like Fidelity, J.P. Morgan, Perplexity, American Express, Notion, Rippling, Hubspot, and 100+ others. Business Insider dubbed me the "Gatsby of Silicon Alley" (I've never read the book), and I've been featured on Fast Company, Bloomberg, Fortune Magazine, Morning Brew, Forbes, Axios, Reuters, and AdAge. I'm also a partner at Next Wave NYC, a venture capital fund. I like to invest in software companies that improve the way we live, work, and connect with other humans.
